I’ve reviewed hundreds of React components over the past four years. In that time, one pattern has caused more performance regressions, memory leaks, and subtle bugs than anything else: `useEffect` used as a general-purpose “run this code when things change” mechanism.
Not because `useEffect` is bad. Because most developers don’t have a precise mental model for what it actually does. I didn’t either, until an incident in production forced me to sit down and really understand it.
## The Incident
We had a dashboard component that fetched loan application data, applied some client-side filtering, and rendered a table. On low-end devices — Android phones used by our field agents — the component would occasionally lock up the browser thread for 800ms to 1.2 seconds on filter changes.
I profiled it. The React DevTools flame graph showed the component re-rendering 6 to 8 times in response to a single filter change. Each render triggered a `useEffect`, which updated state, which triggered another render, which triggered another `useEffect`.
We had built an effect chain without realizing it.
## What useEffect Actually Does
